articles.title: Semaglutide Effective in Adults Aged Over 65 Years Old
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says semaglutide was effective in adults older than 65, with benefits for weight loss and cardiometabolic health. Based on the limited details provided, it describes outcomes in older adults rather than a mechanistic preclinical study. The item adds to ongoing evidence on GLP-1 drugs in age-specific obesity care.

articles.title: Semaglutide Helps Young Adults with Severe Obesity
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that semaglutide significantly reduced BMI in young adults with treatment-resistant severe obesity. The summary indicates it followed obesity care and focused on a younger patient group. It highlights potential age-specific use of GLP-1 therapy in severe obesity management.

articles.title: Intermittent fasting triggers surprising changes in the brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: In adults with obesity, an intermittent-fasting style diet was linked to weight loss, improved metabolic markers, and shifts in gut bacteria. Brain imaging also showed changes in regions involved in appetite, cravings, and self-control. The findings suggest a gut-brain component to weight-loss responses, though the study does not by itself establish long-term clinical outcomes.

articles.title: This tomato-soy juice reduced inflammation in just four weeks
articles.summary_3_sentences: A clinical study found that adults with obesity who drank a tomato-soy juice daily for four weeks had lower levels of several inflammatory proteins. A control tomato juice did not produce the same effect. The report points to a short-term dietary intervention with anti-inflammatory potential, but it does not establish effects on disease outcomes.

articles.title: Omega-3 fish oil shows promise against type 2 diabetes
articles.summary_3_sentences: This preclinical study in diabetic rats found that omega-3 supplementation improved blood sugar, cholesterol, and inflammatory measures. The summary says the effect may involve shifting immune cells toward a less inflammatory state. These findings are mechanistic and animal-based, so they do not directly establish benefit in people with type 2 diabetes.

articles.title: Beyond THC: Full Cannabis Extract Improves Glucose Levels
articles.summary_3_sentences: This item reports that multiple compounds in cannabis may contribute to glucose regulation. The limited summary emphasizes metabolic effects beyond THC alone. Details are sparse, so the piece should be interpreted as an early report rather than definitive clinical guidance.

articles.title: Yo-yo Diet Fears May Be Unfounded
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article says repeated cycles of weight loss and regain may be less harmful than previously believed. The summary is brief and does not provide study design or endpoints. It reflects ongoing debate about the long-term health effects of weight cycling.

articles.title: Scientists discover why Ozempic and Wegovy weight loss eventually plateaus
articles.summary_3_sentences: NIH researchers reported that semaglutide triggers differing responses in appetite-regulating brain cells, offering a possible explanation for weight-loss plateaus. They also identified a potential strategy to extend drug effects. The work adds mechanistic insight into variable responses to GLP-1 therapy.

articles.title: AI scans 400,000 Reddit posts and finds hidden Ozempic side effects
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers used AI to analyze more than 400,000 Reddit posts about GLP-1 drugs and identified frequently discussed symptoms such as menstrual changes, chills, and hot flashes. The study suggests social media analysis could complement formal pharmacovigilance. Because the signals come from self-reported online posts, they should be treated as hypothesis-generating rather than proof of causation.

articles.title: Wegovy linked to rare "eye stroke" that can cause sudden blindness
articles.summary_3_sentences: This analysis of FDA adverse-event reports found a stronger safety signal for a rare ischemic eye event with Wegovy than with Ozempic. Both drugs contain semaglutide, but the report says the signal was higher for the obesity indication product. As with all spontaneous reporting analyses, the finding indicates a signal that needs further study and does not prove risk magnitude or causality.

articles.title: Popular GLP-1 weight-loss drugs like Ozempic slash heart attack and stroke risk
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large international review found that GLP-1 drugs were associated with lower risks of heart attack, stroke, heart failure, and premature death over time. The report frames these medicines as having cardiovascular benefits beyond weight loss and diabetes control. Interpretation still depends on the quality and mix of the underlying studies included in the review.

articles.title: New "Trojan horse" obesity drug supercharges weight loss in early tests
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers developed a next-generation obesity drug that combines GLP-1/GIP targeting with delivery of an added metabolic enhancer. In mice, it produced greater weight loss and metabolic improvement than current approaches while using lower doses. The findings are preclinical and early, so their relevance to human treatment remains to be tested.

articles.title: New obesity discovery rewrites decades of fat science
articles.summary_3_sentences: This mechanistic study reports that the fat-regulating protein HSL also has a nuclear role in maintaining adipocyte health. People and mice lacking the protein developed lipodystrophy rather than obesity, challenging simpler models of fat metabolism. The work reframes HSL as a broader regulator of adipose biology.

articles.title: The dark side of weight loss drugs: Ozempic's surprising hidden cost
articles.summary_3_sentences: This study reports that people who lose weight with GLP-1 medications may face greater stigma than those losing weight through diet and exercise. The effect appears linked to perceptions that medication-based weight loss is an "easy way out." The findings highlight a social and behavioral dimension of obesity treatment rather than a biomedical outcome.

articles.title: Bariatric Surgery Costs Loss than GLP-1 Drugs Over Time
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says bariatric surgery may cost less than ongoing GLP-1 therapy over a two-year period. The available summary focuses on comparative cost rather than safety or effectiveness details. It contributes to policy and health-economics discussions about obesity treatment choices.

articles.title: Younger U.S. women of color face rising breast cancer deaths, new study finds
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers reported rising breast cancer mortality among younger U.S. women of color. The article points to a shift from an older-age concentration of deaths toward younger women. It underscores persistent disparities in cancer outcomes and the need for targeted prevention and care strategies.

articles.title: First comprehensive look at breast cancer in Native American women reveals key genetic differences
articles.summary_3_sentences: This study is described as the first detailed molecular analysis of breast cancer tissue from Native American women. Researchers found genetic differences that may affect how well therapies work in this population. The work highlights an important gap in precision oncology data across underrepresented groups.

articles.title: Experimental pill promises new hope for deadly pancreatic cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers reported that an experimental pill helped people with advanced pancreatic cancer live longer. The article presents the result as an encouraging development for a cancer with limited treatment success. Further details on trial size, comparator, and safety are not provided in the summary.

articles.title: Long-term leukemia trial reveals MRD-triggered treatment may slow or prevent relapse
articles.summary_3_sentences: Long-term results from the RELAZA2 study suggest that minimal residual disease-guided treatment may delay or prevent relapse in MDS and AML. The report describes it as the first prospective MRD-triggered study in these conditions. It supports the use of molecular monitoring to guide earlier intervention in hematologic malignancy.

articles.title: New T-cell engager shows promise for advanced solid tumors
articles.summary_3_sentences: A first-in-human clinical study tested the bispecific T-cell engager IMA401 in patients with advanced solid tumors. The therapy is designed to link T cells to tumor cells expressing MAGEA4/8 and CD3. The report highlights the approach as an early immunotherapy strategy for otherwise difficult-to-treat cancers.

articles.title: Gene test can safely spare many breast cancer patients of chemotherapy
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large international trial found that a gene test could identify breast cancer patients who can avoid chemotherapy without increasing recurrence risk. The result suggests more personalized treatment and less exposure to unnecessary toxicity. It is a clinically relevant advance in treatment de-escalation.

articles.title: Dual PET imaging detects tumor progression and heart inflammation during cancer treatment
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers developed a dual PET imaging method that tracks tumor progression and cardiac inflammation during cancer therapy at the same time. The report says the approach was used to show a combination therapy that improved tumor response while reducing heart inflammation. The work links oncology imaging with cardio-oncology monitoring.

articles.title: Study highlights major gaps in online info for patients about AI and cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: Research presented at ASCO found that online information for patients about AI in cancer care is limited and often low quality. Available materials were described as difficult to read and likely to omit potential risks. The study points to a communication gap as AI tools move further into oncology practice.

articles.title: New AI approach aims to predict radiation dose before therapy in advanced prostate cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers created a machine-learning approach to estimate radiation dose to tumors and healthy organs before PSMA-targeted therapy begins in advanced prostate cancer. The tool uses pre-therapy PET/CT data and may help personalize treatment planning. If validated, it could improve patient selection and reduce toxicity risk.

articles.title: Antibody-Drug Conjugate Improves Survival for Endometrial Cancer Patients
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says an antibody-drug conjugate improved survival in endometrial cancer patients. The available summary offers limited details on the trial or comparator treatment. It points to continued momentum for targeted drug-delivery approaches in gynecologic oncology.

articles.title: Scientists supercharge natural killer cells to fight aggressive cancers
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that temporarily blocking two proteins made NK cells more effective against several aggressive cancers. The work included preclinical models involving leukemia, glioblastoma, kidney cancer, and triple-negative breast cancer. It suggests a way to enhance innate immune cell activity in cancer therapy.

articles.title: Scientists say house cats could help unlock new cancer treatments for humans
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large comparative oncology study genetically analyzed nearly 500 cat tumors and found similarities with cancers in dogs and humans. Shared cancer-driving genes were noted, including genes relevant to aggressive breast cancers. The findings support using naturally occurring animal cancers to inform translational cancer research.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden weakness shared by hundreds of cancer mutations
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers introduced PerturbFate, a tool designed to track how many different mutations reshape cells over time and converge on shared control pathways. The concept could help identify common vulnerabilities across cancers with diverse genetic drivers. The report frames it as a platform for mutation-heavy diseases rather than a ready-to-use therapy.

articles.title: Engineered Immunotherapy Improves Brain Cancer Outcomes
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports improved outcomes with an engineered immunotherapy approach for brain cancer. The summary is limited and does not provide tumor type, study model, or effect size. It nonetheless reflects active work on immune-based strategies for hard-to-treat brain tumors.

articles.title: Asthma Drug Shows Promise for Aggressive Cancers
articles.summary_3_sentences: This piece says the asthma drug montelukast may be repurposed for aggressive cancers. The summary is brief, so the stage of evidence and cancer types are not fully clear. It represents a drug-repurposing angle in oncology research.

articles.title: Clinical Trial Endpoints for Metastases-Directed Therapies
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article discusses endpoint selection for trials of metastases-directed therapies in oligometastatic cancer. The focus is methodological and clinical-research oriented rather than on one new treatment result. It highlights how trial design can influence evaluation of cancer interventions.

articles.title: A rare cancer-fighting plant compound has been decoded
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists identified two plant enzymes that produce mitraphylline, a rare natural compound with reported anticancer potential. Solving the biosynthetic pathway could make production more practical and sustainable. This is a basic science advance that may support future drug development rather than immediate treatment use.

articles.title: New drugs could wipe out the "zombie cells" linked to cancer and aging
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that senescent cells persisting after chemotherapy depend on GPX4 for survival, creating a possible therapeutic target. In mice, new drugs that disrupted this protection reduced tumors and improved survival. The study connects senescence biology with cancer treatment resistance in a preclinical setting.

topic_groups.topic: Neuroscience, Brain Disorders and Mental Health

articles.title: PET suggests potential chronic traumatic encephalopathy biomarker in living humans
articles.summary_3_sentences: A first-in-class PET approach is reported to detect a biomarker for chronic traumatic encephalopathy in living people. CTE diagnosis has largely depended on postmortem confirmation, so a live-imaging biomarker would be a major step for research and trials. The article presents the method as promising rather than fully established for routine diagnosis.

articles.title: How stress hormone shapes brain development: New clues to why early plasticity fades
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified a mechanism by which stress hormone signaling may help regulate closure of early-life critical periods in the brain. The work offers mechanistic clues to how developmental plasticity narrows over time. It is basic neuroscience with possible relevance to development and learning disorders.

articles.title: How aging reshapes sensorimotor learning: Older adults may lose explicit strategy but gain implicit adaptation
articles.summary_3_sentences: This study examines how aging affects sensorimotor learning, beyond the well-known decline in motor control. The title suggests older adults may lose explicit strategic control while retaining or gaining implicit adaptation. It adds nuance to how learning and compensation change in later life.

articles.title: Most new moms get the baby blues. But it could be something more serious: postpartum depression
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article distinguishes the common postpartum "baby blues" from postpartum depression, a more serious condition. It appears to be a public-facing health explainer grounded in a real clinical issue. The piece emphasizes recognition of symptoms and the need for appropriate care after childbirth.

articles.title: Scientists found the hidden switch fueling alzheimer's brain inflammation
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified a chemical modification of STING that may keep immune signaling overactive in Alzheimer's disease. The study links this mechanism to harmful neuroinflammation and damage to neuronal connections. It is mechanistic work that may inform future therapeutic targeting of brain inflammation.

articles.title: Caffeine reversed memory problems caused by sleep deprivation
articles.summary_3_sentences: Laboratory research found that sleep deprivation disrupted a brain circuit involved in social memory and that caffeine restored signaling in that pathway. The effect was described as targeted rather than broadly stimulatory. Because the report reflects experimental neuroscience, its implications for human sleep loss need further confirmation.

articles.title: Protein traffic jams may explain aging, memory loss, and Alzheimer's
articles.summary_3_sentences: Using killifish, researchers found that ribosomes increasingly stall and collide with age, potentially driving faulty protein buildup. The mechanism may help explain aspects of brain aging and disorders such as Alzheimer's. This is a basic aging-neuroscience study, not a direct therapeutic trial.

articles.title: New depression treatment targets the immune system instead of the brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: A small clinical trial found that an anti-inflammatory drug used for rheumatoid arthritis may help some people with treatment-resistant depression. Participants reportedly also improved in fatigue, anxiety, and quality of life. The results are early and suggest immune modulation as a possible adjunct path in depression care.

articles.title: CBD may slow Alzheimer's by calming the brain's immune system
articles.summary_3_sentences: In Alzheimer's mice, inhaled CBD reduced markers of neuroinflammation. The report suggests the compound acted by calming overactive immune responses in the brain. These are preclinical findings in animals and do not establish efficacy in people with Alzheimer's disease.

articles.title: Human organoids reveal how to reverse "irreversible" nerve damage
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers built human brain-and-spinal-cord organoid systems and found that neuronal regenerative ability declines during development. They identified a gene network involved and reported that an existing hormone drug boosted regrowth. The work is translational and lab-based, offering a platform for nerve-repair research rather than a ready clinical treatment.

articles.title: Researchers block key protein that helps Parkinson's spread through the brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified GPNMB as a protein that may help propagate Parkinson's-related pathology between cells. Antibodies against the protein blocked the process in early experiments. The findings are preclinical but may open a new avenue for slowing disease spread.

articles.title: Scientists thought brain inflammation was driving long COVID but the scans told a different story
articles.summary_3_sentences: A brain imaging study found no widespread neuroinflammation in people with long COVID. Instead, worse symptoms were associated with increased activity in brain regions linked to mood and emotion. The report refines hypotheses about the neurological basis of persistent post-COVID symptoms.

articles.title: Scientists create supercharged vitamin K that helps the brain heal itself
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists created vitamin K-based compounds that promoted neural stem cells to become neurons more efficiently than natural vitamin K. The work is positioned as a possible future route toward neuroregeneration in disorders such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's. It remains an early-stage discovery rather than a clinical treatment.

articles.title: Popular anti-aging drug combo caused severe brain damage in mice
articles.summary_3_sentences: Mouse research found that a drug combination under study for anti-aging caused severe myelin loss and brain changes. The affected cells resembled those implicated in multiple sclerosis. This is a cautionary preclinical result that may influence how candidate longevity interventions are evaluated for neurological toxicity.

articles.title: USC scientists discover a hidden Alzheimer's trigger and a possible way to shut it down
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified cPLA2 as a possible driver of Alzheimer-related inflammation, especially in people carrying APOE4. They also described candidate compounds that may reduce this inflammatory signaling. The work is preclinical and mechanistic, aimed at identifying new drug targets.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden driver of aging -- Simple supplement reversed brain decline
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice, declining Menin in the hypothalamus was linked to inflammation, memory impairment, and other aging features. Restoring Menin or supplementing D-serine improved some of these changes. The findings are preclinical and suggest a hypothalamic contribution to brain aging.

articles.title: Scientists "recharge" damaged nerves to ease chronic pain
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers reported that supplying healthy mitochondria to damaged nerves reduced chronic pain signals in experimental work. The concept treats nerve dysfunction through cellular energy repair rather than conventional analgesia. This is early mechanistic research and has not yet established clinical benefit in patients.

articles.title: Ultrasound aimed at the brain offers new hope for Parkinson's patients
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report covers high-intensity focused ultrasound as a noninvasive treatment for tremor, stiffness, and pain in Parkinson's disease. It describes symptom relief without conventional surgery. The article reflects growing interest in device-based neuromodulation for movement disorders.

articles.title: New tools may help diagnose Parkinson's earlier than ever
articles.summary_3_sentences: Science News reviews emerging tools for earlier Parkinson's diagnosis, including pens and chemical analyses such as earwax evaluation. The piece suggests that multiple nontraditional biomarkers may eventually aid earlier detection. It is an overview of an evolving diagnostic field rather than one definitive study.

articles.title: Scientists found a hidden Alzheimer's trigger and shut it down
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified the enzyme IDOL as a potential Alzheimer's target and found that removing it from neurons reduced amyloid plaques. The intervention also improved processes tied to neuronal resilience and communication. The work is mechanistic and preclinical, offering another possible pathway for therapeutic development.

articles.title: Scientists discover why Alzheimer's risk hits women so much harder
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study of more than 17,000 adults found that women may be more sensitive than men to common dementia risk factors. The authors suggest prevention strategies may need to be sex-specific. The article focuses on risk pattern differences rather than a new treatment.

articles.title: Scientists reverse Alzheimer's in mice with breakthrough nanotechnology
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice, engineered nanoparticles were reported to restore brain clearance processes, reduce amyloid burden, and improve behavior. The therapy also appeared to repair the blood-brain barrier. These findings are preclinical and should not be interpreted as evidence of reversal in humans.

articles.title: Scientists reversed memory loss by recharging the brain's tiny engines
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers developed a tool to boost mitochondrial activity in the brain and restored memory performance in mouse models of dementia. The results support the idea that cellular energy failure may contribute early to cognitive decline. This is animal-model research aimed at mechanism and future target discovery.

articles.title: Scientists find hidden brain nutrient deficit that may fuel anxiety
articles.summary_3_sentences: A brain imaging analysis found lower choline levels in people with anxiety disorders, especially in the prefrontal cortex. The study suggests a measurable neurochemical pattern associated with anxiety. It does not establish whether low choline is a cause, consequence, or treatment target.

articles.title: The brain's "feel good" chemical may be secretly fueling tinnitus
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice, researchers identified a serotonin-linked brain circuit associated with tinnitus-like behavior. The work may help explain why some people report worse ringing while taking SSRIs. These findings are preclinical and provide a mechanistic clue rather than immediate treatment guidance.

articles.title: New psychedelic-like drugs could treat depression without making you trip
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers created new serotonin-active compounds that enhanced plasticity-related signaling but did not produce hallucinogenic-like behavior in animal tests. The discovery may support development of new treatments for depression, PTSD, or addiction with a different side-effect profile. It remains early-stage, preclinical work.

articles.title: Your "um" and pauses could reveal early dementia risk
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers used AI to analyze natural speech and found that pauses, fillers, and word-finding difficulty tracked with executive function. The work suggests conversation patterns could become a simple screening signal for early cognitive decline. It is a promising diagnostic concept that still requires validation in clinical populations.

articles.title: This daily habit could lower dementia risk by 35%, scientists say
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large long-term observational study linked drinking two to three cups of coffee daily with lower dementia risk, particularly before age 75. The effect appeared to plateau at higher intakes. As an observational finding, it suggests an association rather than proving a protective causal effect.

articles.title: Brain scans reveal a shocking difference between psychopaths and other people
articles.summary_3_sentences: MRI research found that people with psychopathic traits had a larger striatum on average than those without such traits. The study links this brain region to reward, motivation, and impulsive behavior. It is a structural association study and does not determine how these differences arise.

articles.title: MIT scientists discover millions of "silent synapses" in the adult brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: MIT researchers reported that about 30% of synapses in the adult cortex may be "silent" and available for rapid activation during learning. This challenges the idea that such synapses are mainly a feature of early development. The study provides basic insight into memory formation and adult brain plasticity.

articles.title: This simple amino acid supplement greatly reduces Alzheimer's damage
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that oral arginine reduced amyloid buildup, neuroinflammation, and behavioral deficits in animal models of Alzheimer's disease. The report frames arginine as inexpensive and already considered safe, but the evidence here is preclinical. Human efficacy and dosing remain uncertain.

articles.title: This simple blood test might detect depression before symptoms appear
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study found that accelerated aging signatures in monocytes were linked to emotional and cognitive symptoms of depression. The authors propose that this pattern could support a blood-based early detection approach. It is a biomarker study and not yet a diagnostic test ready for routine use.

articles.title: Scientists found the brain doesn't start blank, it starts full
articles.summary_3_sentences: This developmental neuroscience study found that early hippocampal networks begin dense and become more efficient through pruning. The findings challenge the idea that memory circuits start sparse and build up from near-zero. It provides a basic framework for thinking about how early neural wiring supports learning.

articles.title: Boosting one protein helps the brain fight Alzheimer's
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers increased Sox9 to activate astrocytes and improve plaque clearance in mice with Alzheimer's-like pathology. The intervention reduced plaque burden and preserved cognitive function over time. This is preclinical work focused on boosting the brain's own support-cell responses.

articles.title: The heart's 'natural bypass' that could spare patients from risky procedures
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers describe a built-in coronary collateral or "natural bypass" system that may predict whether heart muscle remains viable. The finding could help identify patients who might avoid more invasive procedures. The article suggests a prognostic and decision-support role rather than a new therapy itself.

articles.title: One-time gene editing treatment lowers 'bad' cholesterol by up to 62%
articles.summary_3_sentences: Patients in a study received a one-time gene-editing therapy that lowered LDL cholesterol by as much as 62%. The report emphasizes the potential of a single-infusion approach for long-term lipid control. As an early clinical development, longer-term safety and durability remain important open questions.

articles.title: Cryoprobe boosts lung biopsy accuracy by nearly 10 points over forceps
articles.summary_3_sentences: A JAMA study found that transbronchial lung biopsy using a cryoprobe had a higher diagnostic yield than forceps. The result applied to pulmonary nodules or masses, recent lung transplant, and diffuse parenchymal disease. The finding may influence procedural choices in pulmonary diagnostics.

articles.title: New analysis finds geographical differences in access to donor lungs, transplants
articles.summary_3_sentences: This study found that geography still affects access to donor lungs in the United States despite allocation system changes. The article points to continuing inequities in transplant access. It is a health-systems and policy finding with implications for organ allocation reform.

articles.title: AI repurposes routine chest X-rays to catch silent bone loss before fracture
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers are using AI to extract osteoporosis risk information from routine chest X-rays. The goal is to identify people with silent bone loss who may be missed by current screening guidelines. It is a diagnostic triage concept that could broaden opportunistic screening if validated.

articles.title: A silent kidney crisis is spreading far faster than experts expected
articles.summary_3_sentences: A global analysis reported that chronic kidney disease affects nearly 800 million people and is a leading cause of death. The disease is often silent early and contributes substantially to cardiovascular risk. The report underscores CKD as a major and potentially underestimated public health burden.

articles.title: Beans and Soy May Lower Blood Pressure
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article says higher intake of beans and soy may improve heart health by lowering blood pressure. The brief summary suggests a dietary prevention angle rather than an intervention trial. Specific study methods are not given in the provided text.

articles.title: Eating more beans and soy could slash high blood pressure risk by nearly 30%
articles.summary_3_sentences: A broad analysis found that high intake of legumes and soy foods was associated with lower hypertension risk. The report gives estimated risk reductions of 16% for legumes and 19% for soy foods. These are association-based findings that support dietary prevention strategies rather than proving direct causation.

articles.title: Beet juice lowers blood pressure in older adults in just 2 weeks
articles.summary_3_sentences: In older adults, concentrated beetroot juice taken twice daily for two weeks lowered blood pressure and altered oral bacteria. Younger adults did not show the same effect. The study suggests a microbiome-linked dietary approach to blood pressure control in aging.

articles.title: Common heart drug taken by millions found useless -- and possibly dangerous
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large international study found no clear benefit of beta blockers after uncomplicated heart attack in patients with preserved heart function. The report also notes a possible higher risk of adverse outcomes in women receiving the drugs. If confirmed, the findings could affect long-standing prescribing practices after myocardial infarction.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden liver switch that cuts harmful cholesterol
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified HELZ2 as a liver protein that suppresses apoB production and may reduce cholesterol export into the bloodstream. The work reveals a new regulatory layer in lipid metabolism. It is mechanistic science that could inform future cardiovascular drug development.

articles.title: This 800-year-old Chinese exercise helps lower blood pressure naturally
articles.summary_3_sentences: A clinical trial found that baduanjin, a gentle traditional mind-body exercise, lowered blood pressure in adults with stage 1 hypertension. The effect appeared within three months and lasted up to a year. The study suggests a low-cost nonpharmacologic option for some patients with mild hypertension.

articles.title: A common constipation drug shows surprising power to protect kidneys
articles.summary_3_sentences: In a clinical trial of 150 patients with moderate CKD, lubiprostone was associated with better preservation of kidney function. Researchers linked the effect to gut microbiome changes and increased spermidine production. The study suggests a gut-kidney therapeutic pathway worth further investigation.

articles.title: Breakthrough biomaterial heals tissue from the inside out
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists developed an injectable biomaterial that can be delivered intravenously and then localize to damaged tissue. In animal studies, it improved repair after heart attack and showed promise in traumatic brain injury and pulmonary hypertension models. This is a translational biomaterials advance that remains preclinical.

topic_groups.topic: Infectious Diseases and Public Health Threats

articles.title: Two patients with Ebola-like symptoms in isolation in Brazil
articles.summary_3_sentences: Brazilian officials isolated two recently arrived patients with Ebola-like symptoms. The report reflects a containment response to a potential high-consequence infectious disease event. At this stage, it describes suspected cases rather than confirmed transmission.

articles.title: What to know about the outbreak of a rare kind of Ebola
articles.summary_3_sentences: This overview article describes an outbreak of a rare Ebola virus type in Congo and Uganda. It notes that cases are outpacing the response. The piece serves as a public health explainer on a fast-moving outbreak situation.

articles.title: Murray Valley encephalitis can be fatal. With no vaccine, here's how to stay safe
articles.summary_3_sentences: After deaths in Alice Springs, health authorities warned residents and visitors to avoid mosquito bites because no vaccine is available for Murray Valley encephalitis. The article centers on prevention and risk communication. It highlights vector control and personal protection as the main tools currently available.

articles.title: Why some chikungunya virus infections may turn chronic
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article examines why some chikungunya infections may progress from acute illness to chronic symptoms. It points to ongoing research into mechanisms behind persistent joint pain and inflammation. The piece is relevant as chikungunya resurges in multiple countries.

articles.title: Why is hantavirus so deadly? It's not what you may think
articles.summary_3_sentences: Science News reports that Andes hantavirus causes fatal lung failure through mechanisms distinct from many other respiratory infections. Understanding this disease biology could help guide future treatments. The article focuses on pathogenesis rather than a new clinical intervention.

articles.title: Congo prepared for Ebola. Now a rare strain is exposing gaps in readiness
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says Congo's Bundibugyo Ebola outbreak is exposing preparedness gaps despite prior Ebola experience. Public health teams are relying on older outbreak-control tactics while new tools are still being sought. The article highlights the challenge of responding to rare strains with fewer ready-made countermeasures.

articles.title: How house design can curb childhood illnesses in Africa
articles.summary_3_sentences: Experimental houses in Tanzania reduced malaria, diarrhea, and infections in children through features such as screens, ventilation, and rainwater systems. The report highlights housing design as a preventive public health intervention. It shows how built environment changes can affect infectious disease risk.

articles.title: Screening all kids for type 1 diabetes can catch more cases early
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that broader screening for type 1 diabetes in children can identify more early cases. Early detection may improve outcomes by recognizing disease before severe presentation. The item focuses on population screening strategy rather than treatment.

articles.title: Scientists sound alarm as dangerous amoebas spread globally
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers warn that free-living amoebae are becoming a greater global health concern as warming and aging water systems favor their spread. Some species can cause severe infections and also shelter other harmful microbes. The article calls for improved surveillance and water treatment rather than presenting one new intervention.

articles.title: Influencers are promoting dangerous peptides on social media, and regulators are struggling to keep up
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report describes how unproven injectable peptides are being promoted by influencers to mainstream audiences online. Regulators are portrayed as struggling to respond to the speed and scale of the market. The article focuses on consumer safety and the digital spread of poorly regulated products.

articles.title: Hidden driving danger when edible cannabis and alcohol mix
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that combining cannabis edibles with alcohol impaired driving-related performance more than either substance alone. Standard field sobriety tests also missed some cannabis-related impairment. The work highlights a public safety concern around combined substance use.

articles.title: Study Explores the Effect of Cannabis Exposure on Toddler Cognitive Development
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that children exposed to cannabis in utero showed no disruptions in cognitive or verbal development in the study described. The provided summary is brief and does not include design details or confounding factors. It should therefore be read as a limited report rather than a definitive conclusion about prenatal cannabis exposure.

articles.title: Cannabis capsules ease severe chronic arthritis pain
articles.summary_3_sentences: This item says cannabis capsules eased severe chronic arthritis pain in a study published in Clinical Therapeutics. The short summary does not provide dose, comparator, or safety details. It adds to ongoing interest in cannabinoid-based pain management.

articles.title: Study Compared the Risks of Vaporizing and Smoking Cannabis
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article summarizes a study comparing the risks of vaporizing herbal cannabis versus smoking it. The available text is limited and does not specify outcomes or whether one route was safer overall. It reflects continuing research on harm differences between cannabis delivery methods.

articles.title: Medical Cannabis Linked to Lower Opioid Use for Pain
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says medical cannabis use was linked to lower opioid use among people treated for pain. The wording suggests an association rather than proof that cannabis caused opioid reduction. Details on study design and possible confounding are not included in the provided summary.

articles.title: The Effects of Combined Edible and Alcohol Consumption on Driving
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that consuming cannabis edibles together with alcohol significantly increases driving impairment. It aligns with broader evidence that combined intoxicants can have additive or greater-than-additive effects. The summary is brief but clearly frames the issue as a safety concern.

articles.title: Is cannabis safe after 65? Stanford experts reveal 5 risks older adults should know
articles.summary_3_sentences: Experts from Stanford warn that modern cannabis may pose underrecognized risks for older adults, including falls, heart issues, memory problems, drug interactions, and addiction. The article is an expert overview rather than a single new trial. It highlights that increasing potency may alter the risk profile for aging users.

articles.title: Don't toss cannabis leaves: Scientists found rare compounds with medical potential
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified dozens of previously unrecognized compounds in cannabis leaves, including rare flavoalkaloids. The discovery expands the known chemistry of the plant and may support future pharmacology work. It is basic natural-products research, not evidence of clinical benefit.

articles.title: Sweet Success: Licorice Compound Shows Promise for IBD
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that glycyrrhizin, a compound from black licorice, showed promise for inflammatory bowel disease. The summary suggests therapeutic potential but provides limited details on the experimental setting. It fits with a growing pipeline of natural-compound approaches for gut inflammation.

articles.title: Microplastics altered allergy-related immune responses after 14 days in mouse lungs
articles.summary_3_sentences: A mouse study found that PET microplastics remained in the lungs, worsened airway inflammation, and changed allergy-related immune responses after 14 days. The work is preclinical and focused on immune effects in an animal model. It adds mechanistic evidence to concerns about inhaled microplastic exposure.

articles.title: Endocannabinoid drop links to severe acute pancreatitis, offering possible treatment target
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified alterations in the endocannabinoid system associated with the most severe acute pancreatitis. The finding suggests a potential biomarker or treatment target for severe disease. The article is mechanistic and does not yet describe a tested therapy.

articles.title: Mitochondria Regulates Immune Response
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reviews how mitochondrial activity influences immune signaling pathways. It emphasizes that changes in mitochondrial function can modulate immune responses and may offer therapeutic targets. The piece appears to be a broad science report rather than coverage of one narrow clinical study.

articles.title: Evaluating Spatial Transcriptomics Platforms for IBD Clinics & Research
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article compares spatial transcriptomics platforms for studying inflammatory bowel disease tissue. It outlines strengths and limitations of different systems for clinical and research applications. The piece is methodological and relevant to translational GI research infrastructure.

articles.title: MIT scientists discover amino acid that helps the gut heal itself
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice, a cysteine-rich diet activated immune cells that released signals helping intestinal stem cells repair radiation-damaged gut tissue. The findings suggest a diet-immune-stem cell pathway in intestinal healing. This is preclinical work with potential relevance to cancer treatment-related gut injury.

articles.title: Scientists discover tiny gut particles that may drive aging and chronic disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers report that microscopic gut-derived particles may contribute to inflammation and chronic disease with aging. Particles from young animals appeared to reduce some age-related damage in older animals. The study is preclinical and explores a new inflammatory pathway linking gut biology and aging.

articles.title: Black licorice compound shows promise against inflammatory bowel disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers used a stem cell-based human intestine model to screen compounds and identified glycyrrhizin as a promising anti-inflammatory candidate for IBD. In lab-grown tissue and mice, it reduced intestinal damage and cell death. This is translational preclinical work that may guide future drug development.

articles.title: Scientists discover a new way to prevent gum disease without killing good bacteria
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that blocking communication signals among dental plaque bacteria shifted the oral microbiome toward healthier species. This approach reduced disease-associated microbes without broadly killing bacteria. The work suggests a more selective strategy for preventing gum disease.

articles.title: A Natural Compound That May Disarm Drug-Resistant Bacteria
articles.summary_3_sentences: This piece describes a natural compound that may weaken drug-resistant bacteria rather than simply trying to kill them outright. The summary is limited but suggests an anti-resistance mechanism. It points to ongoing efforts to develop new antimicrobial strategies in the face of resistance.

topic_groups.topic: Genetics, Cell Biology and Regenerative Science

articles.title: The Evolution of Blood Cells
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reviews the origin and differentiation of blood cells and recent discoveries about their evolution. It places hematopoiesis in a broader evolutionary context. The piece is a general science report relevant to hematology and immune system biology.

articles.title: Epigenetics Break the Bounds of Mendelian Inheritance
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article discusses how epigenetic changes can influence inheritance beyond classic Mendelian patterns. It highlights cases in which altered gene expression may be passed across generations. The piece is conceptual and broad, emphasizing expanding views of heredity.

articles.title: Scientists say evolution may work differently than we thought
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers challenge the long-standing view that most fixed genetic changes are neutral, arguing that beneficial mutations may be more common than assumed. They suggest that changing environments can prevent such mutations from sweeping through entire populations. The study is evolutionary genetics rather than medical research, but it has broad implications for how adaptation is modeled.

articles.title: Scientists discover ancient single-celled ancestors still live on in your blood
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers traced features of human blood cells back to single-celled ancestors hundreds of millions of years ago. By reconstructing an evolutionary tree, they linked modern immune cell biology to ancient life forms. The work is basic evolutionary cell biology with implications for understanding hematologic function.

articles.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
articles.summary_3_sentences: This study examined cells that duplicate their DNA but fail to divide, producing polyploid-like states linked to cancer and aging. Researchers found that not all failed divisions lead to the same fate. The work adds detail to how abnormal cell-cycle events may persist rather than trigger cell death.

articles.title: Scientists use light to create tiny molecules that could transform medicine
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers developed a photocatalytic method to make highly strained housane molecules useful in medicinal chemistry. The work solves a synthetic challenge by guiding the reaction toward a clean pathway. It is a chemistry advance that may expand future drug-design options.

articles.title: Scientists found the "holy grail" gene that could one day help humans regrow limbs
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers studying axolotls, zebrafish, and mice identified shared regeneration-related genes important for bone regrowth. Disabling these genes impaired regeneration, while a zebrafish-inspired gene therapy partially restored it in mice. The work is preclinical and highly translational, but far from human limb regrowth applications.

articles.title: Scientists accidentally discover DNA that breaks the rules of life
articles.summary_3_sentences: A sequencing experiment uncovered a protist with an unusual genetic code that changes how gene termination signals are interpreted. The finding challenges assumptions about the near-universality of the genetic code. It is a basic biology discovery with broad implications for molecular evolution.

articles.title: Scientists successfully transfer longevity gene and extend lifespan
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers transferred a naked mole rat longevity-related gene into mice and observed healthier aging and longer lifespan. The gene increased high-molecular-weight hyaluronic acid, which was linked to reduced inflammation and cancer resistance. This is animal research and not evidence that the strategy is ready for people.

topic_groups.topic: Clinical Practice, Women's Health and Lifestyle

articles.title: Vitamin D levels stay strong in Swedish children after 2018 fortification changes
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study of more than 1,800 Swedish infants found overall good vitamin D status after fortification changes, with no indications of deficiency. However, some children still had low intake. The article also notes tension between nutrient sources and greenhouse gas emissions, adding a sustainability dimension.

articles.title: Early interventions can help women achieve optimal weight gain during pregnancy, review suggests
articles.summary_3_sentences: A review found that early interventions may help women achieve healthier weight gain during pregnancy. The article emphasizes risks associated with both too little and too much gain, especially in low- and middle-income settings. It points to nutrition, health-care access, and supplementation as major factors.

articles.title: There are different types of fitness--exercise expert explains
articles.summary_3_sentences: This expert explainer notes that fitness is multidimensional and task-specific rather than one general trait. Endurance in one activity does not necessarily translate to skill or performance in another. The piece is a general health explainer about exercise and physical capability.

articles.title: Repairing DNA damage: Scientists discover a surprising new benefit of melatonin
articles.summary_3_sentences: An early study suggests melatonin supplementation may increase DNA repair activity in night shift workers. The finding raises the possibility of reducing some biological stress from overnight work. It is preliminary and does not yet show that supplementation prevents long-term disease.

articles.title: Vitamin B12 and folate deficiencies linked to chronic fatigue
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers in Japan found that low vitamin B12 and folate levels were associated with fatigue and low motivation in otherwise healthy people. The study suggests micronutrient status may be relevant in some cases of persistent tiredness. It reports an association and does not prove these deficiencies are the sole cause of symptoms.

articles.title: Forget LASIK: Safer, cheaper vision correction without lasers or surgery
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers are testing a nonlaser approach that temporarily softens the cornea with electrical pulses so it can be reshaped. Early experiments were done in rabbit eyes and corrected nearsightedness quickly while preserving structure. The concept is preclinical and far from replacing established refractive procedures in humans.

articles.title: New drug could finally stop deadly fatty liver disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: Clinical trial results for ION224 suggest the drug may improve liver health in MASH by blocking an enzyme involved in fat buildup and inflammation. The article says benefits were seen even without weight loss. This points to a direct disease-modifying strategy for fatty liver disease, though more data are needed.

articles.title: Scientists are raising new questions about vitamin B12 and cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reviews evidence suggesting that both low and very high vitamin B12 levels may be relevant to cancer risk or outcomes. It argues against a simplistic "more is better" view of supplementation. The piece is cautionary and interpretive rather than reporting one new randomized trial.

articles.title: Surprising research reveals why you shouldn't add bananas to your smoothies
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that adding bananas to berry smoothies reduced absorption of flavanols. The study suggests that food combinations can affect how much of certain plant compounds the body takes up. It is a nutrition-bioavailability finding, not a broad statement against bananas or smoothies in general.

articles.title: Scientists warn popular vitamin D supplement may have a "previously unknown" downside
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study suggests vitamin D2 supplementation may lower vitamin D3 levels, while D3 may be more effective for overall vitamin D status. The report also raises the possibility of immune differences between forms. These findings may influence supplementation choices but require confirmation across broader settings.

articles.title: Scientists warn that current vitamin B12 guidelines may be putting your brain at risk
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that older adults with lower active B12 levels, even within the normal range, had signs of slower thinking and more white-matter damage. The findings suggest current thresholds may miss clinically relevant insufficiency for brain health. This is an observational biomarker study, not direct evidence that changing guidelines will improve outcomes.

articles.title: Scientists uncover cancer-causing chemicals hidden in everyday foods
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article highlights polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in foods exposed to high-heat cooking or contamination. It emphasizes potential long-term cancer risk from exposure. The piece is a public health warning about food chemistry rather than evidence from a new intervention study.

articles.title: Surprising study finds beef doesn't worsen blood sugar or diabetes risk
articles.summary_3_sentences: A short clinical trial in adults with prediabetes found no worsening of glycemic control or inflammatory markers when daily beef intake was compared with poultry. The study challenges a common assumption about this specific dietary substitution over one month. It does not address longer-term outcomes or broader dietary patterns.

articles.title: Scientists discover strange link between vitamin D and pain
articles.summary_3_sentences: Patients with low vitamin D before mastectomy were more likely to experience more severe postoperative pain and use more opioids, according to this study. The report suggests vitamin D status may influence pain processing and inflammation. It is an observational clinical finding that may justify further interventional study.

articles.title: Scientists uncover surprising health benefits of watermelon
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report summarizes studies linking watermelon intake with better overall diet quality and possible vascular benefits from watermelon juice. It presents watermelon as a nutrient-rich food rather than a stand-alone treatment. The evidence includes observational and small intervention-style findings.

articles.title: The real reason exercise makes you stronger isn't what you think
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice, researchers found that certain brain cells remained active after exercise and appeared necessary for endurance gains over time. Blocking these cells prevented improved stamina despite continued exercise. The work suggests a central nervous system contribution to training adaptation.

articles.title: This silent tooth infection could be hurting your whole body
articles.summary_3_sentences: Studies summarized here suggest that chronic tooth-root infections may contribute to inflammation and impaired blood sugar control. Root canal treatment was associated with improved inflammatory markers and glycemic measures in some reports. The article links oral health with broader metabolic health, though causality remains difficult to establish fully.

articles.title: New Dry Eye Treatment Makes Eyes Photosynthesize
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article describes an experimental dry-eye treatment inspired by photosynthesis. The brief summary indicates a novel bioengineering approach but provides few details on the model or stage of testing. It appears to be an early translational concept for ocular surface disease.

articles.title: Weight loss drug Ozempic linked to lower depression and anxiety risk
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large observational study linked semaglutide use with fewer psychiatric hospital visits and sick days related to mental health. The report suggests possible mental health benefits in addition to metabolic effects. Because it is observational, it cannot prove that the drug directly reduces depression or anxiety risk.

articles.title: How a remarkable trial on bedrest during the Korean War led to evidence‑based medicine
articles.summary_3_sentences: This historical article describes how an early trial challenging bed rest influenced the emergence of evidence-based medicine. It centers on David Sackett and the broader shift toward rigorous clinical research in decision-making. The piece is about medical history and methodology rather than a new biomedical finding.

articles.title: The ocean's health may depend on a tiny microbe inside fish
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found evidence that microbes in marine fish may help produce calcium carbonate and influence ocean chemistry. The work revises earlier assumptions that fish alone handled this process. It is an environmental biology story rather than a medical study.

articles.title: The secret to pigeons' incredible navigation was hiding in their liver
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists identified iron-rich immune cells in pigeon livers that may contribute to magnetic sensing. Birds lacking these cells had difficulty navigating under overcast skies. The study offers a new explanation for animal navigation and links immunity with environmental sensing.

articles.title: Why cancer spreads more in middle age than in old age
articles.summary_3_sentences: This preclinical melanoma study found metastasis was lowest in young mice, highest in middle-aged mice, and lower again in very old mice. Researchers point to an immune cell population that may keep cancer dormant and limit spread. The work is mechanistic and age-related, but the evidence described is in mice.

articles.title: This newly discovered raptor may have hunted like a giant heron
articles.summary_3_sentences: Paleontologists describe a newly discovered Patagonian dinosaur, Kank australis, with anatomy suggesting heron-like fishing behavior. The finding revises ideas about how some predatory dinosaurs hunted. It is a paleontology item outside medicine.

articles.title: A 100-year-old piano mystery has finally been solved
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers used high-speed sensing of piano keystrokes to show that touch can alter the perceived tone color of notes. The finding addresses a long-running debate in music performance. It is a sensory and performance science story, not a medical one.

articles.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles' lost crocodiles
articles.summary_3_sentences: DNA from museum specimens showed that extinct Seychelles crocodiles were an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles, not a unique species. The work resolves a long-standing historical zoology question. It is a biodiversity and evolution finding outside health news.

articles.title: New MIND Tool Analyzes Microbiomes to Understand & Alter Them
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report introduces a tool called MIND for analyzing gut microbiomes and identifying patterns linked to health states. The summary also suggests it may support efforts to alter microbiome composition. It is a methods-focused science item rather than a direct clinical result.

articles.title: Venomous Himalayan pit viper was actually 5 different species all along
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists found that the Himalayan pit viper actually comprises five distinct species, including three newly recognized ones. The discovery has implications for biodiversity and possibly venom research. It is primarily a taxonomy and conservation story.

articles.title: Oxygenated Seas Hosted Early Complex Life-Findings Challenge Assumptions
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports evidence that oxygen-rich seas may have supported early complex life earlier than some assumptions suggested. It reframes ideas about the environmental setting for ancient eukaryotes. The piece is focused on evolutionary Earth history.

articles.title: Think you're bad at languages? Experts say these 5 myths are to blame
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article challenges common myths that discourage language learning and argues that communication and cultural engagement matter more than perfection. It emphasizes modern tools and social opportunities for learning. The piece is educational science rather than medical news.

articles.title: How to scout a safe summer swimming hole
articles.summary_3_sentences: Science News offers practical advice for safer swimming, including checking public E. coli reports and avoiding risky exposures. The item is a seasonal public health explainer. It focuses on prevention rather than a new study result.

articles.title: _Morbid_ doesn't want you to fall for antiaging hype
articles.summary_3_sentences: This piece covers a book critiquing high-profile longevity claims and anti-aging medicine. It is an evidence-oriented commentary
